title: End-to-End Pipeline Design
concept: Designing an end-to-end segmentation pipeline requires structuring data preparation, backbone selection, decoder selection, loss matching, and post-processing.

exercises.prompt: Recap: Why is Hausdorff distance a valuable metric?

options.text: It measures the worst-case boundary error, capturing localized contour spikes
It calculates the total number of True Negatives in the background
It is the only metric compatible with 1D convolutions

exercises.explain: Correct! Hausdorff measures the maximum distance between contours.

cards.title: Starting the capstone
cards.body: Welcome to the Phase 9 Capstone! Over the next five lessons, we will design and deploy a complete, real-world image segmentation system from scratch. We begin with the architecture blueprint: how do we connect the data, the backbone, the decoder, and the loss function into a single working pipeline?

cards.title: Pipeline Blueprint
cards.body:
An End-to-End Segmentation Pipeline consists of:
1. **Data Prep** (loading and augmenting masks).
2. **Backbone** (feature extraction).
3. **Decoder** (ASPP or U-Net upsampling).
4. **Loss Function** (BCE + Dice).
5. **Thresholding** (converting probabilities to binary masks).

cards.title: An assembly line
cards.body: Designing a pipeline is like setting up a car factory. You don't just buy an engine; you map how raw steel (data) enters, gets shaped by robots (backbone), painted (decoder), inspected (loss), and finally driven out the gate (inference).

cards.title: Self-driving blueprint
cards.body: A self-driving team builds a road segmentation system. They feed camera video into a ResNet-50 backbone, decode features using an ASPP module, train with Focal Loss, and apply a 0.5 threshold to get the drivable road mask.

cards.title: Don't jump straight to coding
cards.body: You might think the first step is writing PyTorch code. That's a mistake! If you don't map your resolution changes, channel depths, and loss functions on paper first, you will spend days debugging shape mismatch errors during training.

cards.title: End-to-End Pipeline Flow
cards.body: The stages of a segmentation system:

left.text: Raw Image -> ResNet/MobileNet Backbone -> Feature extraction

right.text: Upsampling/Skips -> Sigmoid -> Probability Map -> Threshold -> Final Mask

exercises.prompt: What is the first logical step in designing a segmentation pipeline?

options.text: Structuring data preparation and mapping resolution transitions
Writing the final inference code for mobile devices
Quantizing the model weights to 8-bit integers
Running a CRF post-processor on empty images

exercises.explain: Correct! Mapping resolutions and data prep is the first phase.

exercises.prompt: True or False: The thresholding step converts continuous pixel probabilities (e.g. 0 to 1) into a binary mask (0 or 1).
exercises.explain: True. Thresholding is required to output a clean, finalized mask.

exercises.sentence: The backbone of a segmentation model is responsible for feature ___.
exercises.explain: The backbone extracts high-level semantic features from the input image.

exercises.prompt: Which component is responsible for increasing the spatial resolution back to the input size?
exercises.explain: The decoder upsamples feature maps back to pixel space.

exercises.prompt: You are planning a medical lung nodule pipeline. The scans are low-contrast and have high noise. Which pipeline setup is most logical?

options.text: A U-Net decoder with skip connections to recover fine boundaries, trained using a hybrid BCE + Dice loss.
An FCN-32s with no skip connections, trained using standard unweighted cross-entropy.
A simple classification CNN with no decoder, trained using mean squared error.
A Mask R-CNN with RoIPool, trained using classification loss only.

exercises.explain: Perfect! Medical scans need skip connections to find boundaries, and hybrid loss to handle the class imbalance. This is the classic, reliable pipeline design. Next: handling mask imbalance!
